The match is exact: types and cardinality of every field in the sample line up with the nf-osi BiospecimenTemplate schema.

## Schema Identification

**Best match: `BiospecimenTemplate`** from the nf-osi/nf-metadata-dictionary repository.

**Identifier (`$id`):** `https://repo-prod.prod.sagebase.org/repo/v1/schema/type/registered/org.synapse.nf-biospecimentemplate`

**Source path:** `nf-osi/nf-metadata-dictionary/registered-json-schemas/BiospecimenTemplate.json`

### Why this schema matches

Every field in the sample is present in `BiospecimenTemplate`'s `properties`, with matching types/cardinality:

| Sample field | Sample type | Schema type | Match |
|---|---|---|---|
| `individualID` | array of strings | `array` | ✓ |
| `parentSpecimenID` | string | `string` | ✓ |
| `specimenID` | string | `string` | ✓ |
| `aliquotID` | string | `string` | ✓ |
| `tumorType` | string | `string` | ✓ |

The schema's full property set is `individualID, parentSpecimenID, specimenID, aliquotID, tumorType, bodySite, experimentalCondition, modelSystemName, modelSpecies, modelSex, modelAge, modelAgeUnit` — the sample uses a strict subset, and all three required fields (`individualID`, `specimenID`, `tumorType`) are provided.

### Why the other repository was ruled out

The HTAN v1.1.0 repository contains a `HTAN.BiospecimenData-v1.1.0-schema.json` (title: "Biospecimen", `$id`: `https://w3id.org/htan/biospecimen`), but its property names are uppercase/snake-case (e.g., `HTAN_BIOSPECIMEN_ID`, `HTAN_PARENT_ID`, `TUMOR_CLASSIFICATION`, `BIOSPECIMEN_TYPE`). **None** of the sample's camelCase keys (`individualID`, `parentSpecimenID`, `specimenID`, `aliquotID`, `tumorType`) appear in that schema, so it is not a match. No other schema in either repo's listing is a plausible candidate for this combination of biospecimen identifier fields.

### Confidence note

The identification is based on field-name and type compatibility only — I did not run a formal JSON Schema validator. Since the sample uses placeholder strings (`"sample string"`) rather than real values, any pattern/enum constraints in the schema (e.g., a controlled vocabulary for `tumorType`) would likely fail validation against real values, but the structural match is unambiguous.
